title: Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling
authors: Huajian Ruan, Jinyang Li, Xingyu Guo, Lingxiao Wang
abstract: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.
summary: The authors apply statistical-field methods to analyze text generated by Large Language Models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as a physical system whose temperature is controlled by the softmax parameter $T$. They find evidence of a continuous phase transition at a critical temperature $T_c$, using diagnostics like susceptibility and intrinsic dimension. This suggests that LLM generation possesses an underlying collective statistical structure analogous to physical phase transitions.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the underlying collective statistical structure of text generated by Large Language Models (LLMs) by treating token embeddings as a physical system.
main_result: The analysis reveals clear signatures consistent with a continuous phase transition near a critical temperature $T_c$, evidenced by sharp peaks in susceptibility, changes in an order parameter, and minima in intrinsic dimension.
method: A statistical-field framework is employed, defining observables like susceptibility ($\chi$) and an order parameter ($\langle t angle$), alongside using the Two Nearest Neighbor (TwoNN) method to estimate the system's intrinsic dimension ($I_d$).
model_or_system: The model treats LLM output as a one-dimensional chain of token embeddings, where the softmax temperature $T$ acts analogously to a thermodynamic temperature controlling sampling between deterministic and uniform regimes.
key_observables:
Susceptibility ($\chi$), Order Parameter ($\langle t angle$), Intrinsic Dimension ($I_d$), and critical exponents ($\gamma$, $
u$).
important_parameters: The softmax temperature $T$ is the primary control parameter, with a characteristic critical temperature $T_c$ identified across different model scales.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that LLM generation can be mapped onto an effective statistical mechanics problem, though authors note the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation requires further study.
figures_summary: Figures show $\chi(T)$ peaking near $T_c$, $\langle t angle$ changing sharply at $T_c$, and $I_d$ reaching a minimum near $T_c$ for various model sizes (Qwen3).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the statistical-field framework, applies diagnostics ($\chi, \langle t angle, I_d$) by varying temperature $T$, demonstrates consistent phase transition signatures across multiple models and scales, and concludes with implications for AI trustworthiness.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful analogy between complex machine learning outputs and established concepts from critical phenomena theory, suggesting that LLM behavior can be characterized using tools developed for condensed matter physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Susceptibility χ vs. temperature T for (a) different model scales using Wikipedia prompts (1000 samples, 280 tokens). All models exhibit a peak near Tc ≈1.4, with larger models showing higher peak susceptibility. (b) Different prompt categories generated by Qwen3-32B (1000 samples, 300 tokens).

Figure 2: Power-law fits of susceptibility near Tc for Qwen3-32B. (a) Full χ(T) curve with the fit region highlighted; (b) log-log plot for T > Tc; (c) log-log plot for T < Tc. Both sides yield a critical exponent γ ≈0.1, consistent with power-law divergence.

Figure 3: Stability analysis of the susceptibility signal. (a) Convergence with increasing ensemble size Ns from 2 to 1000 samples; (b) Convergence with increasing sequence length from 100 to 300 tokens.

Figure 4: Order parameter structure via PCA. (a) Projection onto the first two principal components (PC1 explains 82.5%, PC2 explains 10.7%), colored by temperature. Below Tc, ensemble outputs cluster tightly along a single direction; above Tc, they disperse. (b) Magnitude |⟨t⟩| and the first three principal component values vs. temperature, showing a sharp transition near Tc.

Figure 5: Intrinsic dimension Id vs. temperature for five Qwen3 model scales. All models exhibit a minimum near Tc ≈1.3–1.5, after which Id rises sharply and saturates at high T, consistent with the onset of a disordered phase where the configuration space expands.
